refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is performance-focused but lacks explicit binding to safety context and network assumptions. Refinement clarifies that latency is a safety-critical performance requirement tied to operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 percentile metric and network condition qualifier are essential for testability and acceptance.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency (operator input → vehicle sensor/actuator response) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented teleoperation sessions. Collect p95 latency statistics over ≥1000 control cycles per test scenario. Validate against 150 ms threshold under worst-case agreed conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall minimize command transmission latency from operator interface to vehicle control input.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall minimize sensor-to-display latency for operator feedback (video, telemetry).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain latency performance under network degradation (packet loss, jitter) within agreed operational envelope.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and alert when latency exceeds safe operating thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency edge computing architecture with local vehicle autonomy fallback; prioritize command/feedback channels with QoS guarantees; use predictive rendering and sensor fusion to mask network jitter.

DP-B: Deploy dedicated high-bandwidth, low-latency network link (private 5G or satellite); implement real-time OS scheduling on vehicle and operator console; minimize protocol stack overhead via custom transport layer.

DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge autonomy (DP-A) with network optimization (DP-B elements); tiered latency budgets per control loop (critical vs. non-critical); graceful degradation to autonomous mode if latency exceeds threshold.
DP-D: Centralized cloud-based teleoperation with aggressive caching and predictive models; rely on network SLA guarantees; implement operator-side latency compensation via extrapolation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid edge autonomy + network optimization with tiered latency budgets and graceful degradation. Allocate latency budget: network link (60 ms p95), vehicle processing (40 ms p95), operator console (30 ms p95), with autonomous fallback triggered if total exceeds 150 ms.

potential_sources: Coupling between network link optimization (DP-B element) and vehicle autonomy capability (DP-A element) via shared sensor data and control authority handoff logic.
Coupling between operator console latency compensation and vehicle-side predictive models; both must maintain synchronized state.
Coupling between graceful degradation logic and Safety Services (must coordinate safe mode transitions).
coupling_check.notes: Hybrid approach introduces moderate coupling but is justified by safety criticality and operational flexibility. Coupling is manageable via well-defined interfaces (SafetyAlert, Tasking) and explicit state synchronization protocols. Decoupling risk: if autonomy and network optimization are developed independently, latency budget allocation may be violated.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle has sufficient onboard compute and sensor suite to support autonomous fallback mode.
ASSUMPTION: Network link can be provisioned/guaranteed to meet 60 ms p95 target under agreed conditions.
ASSUMPTION: Operator training and worksite safety plan accommodate autonomous mode transitions.
RISK: If vehicle autonomy is immature, graceful degradation may not be safe; fallback to manual mode with higher latency tolerance may be required.
RISK: Network link optimization (5G, satellite) may have high capital/operational cost; cost-benefit analysis needed.
RISK: Latency budget allocation (60/40/30 ms) is preliminary; detailed timing analysis required during DP refinement.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C by explicitly defining: (1) latency budget allocation methodology and sensitivity analysis, (2) autonomous fallback decision logic and safety conditions, (3) network link SLA requirements and contingency plans, (4) operator console latency compensation algorithms. Conduct trade study on network technology (5G vs. satellite vs. terrestrial) against cost, availability, and latency guarantees.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency performance. Encompasses operator console interface, network link management, vehicle-side command processing, sensor feedback aggregation, and latency monitoring/alerting. Responsible for achieving p95 ≤ 150 ms latency budget allocation and graceful degradation logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level mission tasks and operational mode directives from Site Manager/Tele-operator; Teleoperation module translates to low-latency control commands.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and latency metrics to operator console and monitoring systems; includes video feed, position, orientation, load, and diagnostic data.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services; transmits latency-related safety events (e.g., latency threshold exceeded, autonomous mode activated) to Safety Services for coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying network transport (5G, satellite, terrestrial); Teleoperation module manages QoS, prioritization, and latency monitoring over this link.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end latency performance. All latency-critical logic (command processing, feedback aggregation, graceful degradation) is contained. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert coordination and Network Link abstraction cross boundaries.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side command processing and sensor acquisition, but latency optimization (predictive rendering, jitter compensation) and graceful degradation logic belong in Teleoperation. High diffusion risk if latency budget allocation is split between Vehicle and Teleoperation.

Safety Services owns safety policy and constraint enforcement, but latency performance monitoring and optimization are Teleoperation concerns. Assigning latency FR to Safety Services would create high diffusion and coupling.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained for latency ownership. Assigning to NGHE Solution would defer responsibility and create diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it directly controls operator console latency and network link optimization, (2) it owns the latency budget allocation and graceful degradation logic, (3) it interfaces with Safety Services and Vehicle via well-defined SafetyAlert and Tasking channels, (4) it can measure and monitor end-to-end latency as a cohesive performance metric.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle module autonomy capability is underdeveloped, Teleoperation's graceful degradation logic may not have a safe fallback mode.
RISK: If Network Link abstraction is not sufficiently decoupled, Teleoperation module may become tightly coupled to specific network technologies (5G, satellite), reducing flexibility.

RISK: Latency monitoring and alerting logic in Teleoperation must synchronize with Safety Services; if SafetyAlert interface is not well-defined, safety-critical latency events may be missed or delayed.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Vehicle module has sufficient autonomous capability to support graceful degradation. Ensure Network Link interface provides latency metrics and QoS controls without exposing technology-specific details. Define SafetyAlert protocol for latency threshold events with guaranteed delivery and minimal additional latency.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP-C (hybrid edge autonomy + network optimization) directly addresses the refined FR by allocating latency budgets across network, vehicle, and operator console layers, with graceful degradation to autonomous mode. DP-C is feasible, testable, and aligns with safety criticality. Assumptions (vehicle autonomy, network SLA) are documented and manageable.

diffusion_risks: Moderate coupling between Teleoperation and Vehicle via autonomous fallback handoff; mitigated by explicit Tasking interface and SafetyAlert coordination.
Coupling between Teleoperation and Network Link abstraction; mitigated by defining Network Link as a service interface with latency metrics.
Potential diffusion if latency budget allocation is not enforced across module boundaries; recommend explicit latency SLA contracts between Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Network Link.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for latency performance ownership. External dependencies (Vehicle autonomy, Network Link SLA, Safety Services coordination) are minimal and well-defined via interfaces. Ownership is clear and verifiable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Latency FR is directly verifiable via instrumented teleoperation testing. Teleoperation module can measure end-to-end latency (operator input → vehicle response) and report p95 statistics. Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 150 ms) are quantitative and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency budget allocation (60 ms network, 40 ms vehicle, 30 ms operator console) is explicit and traceable to Teleoperation module responsibilities. Each sub-FR (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) maps to specific Teleoperation capabilities (command transmission, sensor feedback, network degradation handling, threshold alerting).

recommended_revisions: Refine latency budget allocation via detailed timing analysis and sensitivity studies; validate that 60/40/30 ms split is achievable with selected network technology and vehicle compute platform.
Define explicit latency SLA contracts between Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Network Link modules; include measurement methodology, reporting frequency, and escalation procedures.
Develop SafetyAlert protocol for latency threshold events; ensure guaranteed delivery and minimal additional latency overhead.
Conduct trade study on network technology (5G, satellite, terrestrial) against latency, cost, availability, and operational constraints; document selection rationale.

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-008 is well-scoped, safety-critical, and non-atomic. It decomposes into four sub-FRs (command transmission latency, sensor feedback latency, network degradation handling, threshold alerting) that require further refinement. Selected DP-C is sound but requires detailed design work (latency budget allocation, autonomous fallback logic, network technology selection). Teleoperation module is the correct owner with 95% containment. Proceeding to next decomposition level is justified to detail sub-FRs and allocate design responsibility.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall minimize command transmission latency from operator interface to vehicle control input (target: ≤40 ms p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall minimize sensor-to-display latency for operator feedback (target: ≤30 ms p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain latency performance under network degradation (packet loss ≤5%, jitter ≤20 ms) within agreed operational envelope.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and alert when latency exceeds safe operating thresholds (≥150 ms p95) and trigger graceful degradation to autonomous mode.

assumptions: Vehicle has sufficient onboard compute and sensor suite to support autonomous fallback mode.
Network link can be provisioned to meet 60 ms p95 latency target under agreed conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
Operator training and worksite safety plan accommodate autonomous mode transitions.
Latency budget allocation (60/40/30 ms) is preliminary and will be refined via detailed timing analysis.
SafetyAlert interface can support latency threshold events with guaranteed delivery.
open_risks: Vehicle autonomy maturity: if insufficient, graceful degradation may not be safe; fallback to manual mode with higher latency tolerance may be required.
Network link cost and availability: 5G/satellite provisioning may be expensive or unavailable in remote worksites; contingency plans needed.
Latency budget allocation sensitivity: if any component (network, vehicle, operator console) cannot meet allocated target, overall p95 ≤ 150 ms goal is at risk.
SafetyAlert protocol overhead: if latency threshold alerting adds significant latency, it may trigger cascading failures; protocol design must be carefully optimized.
Operator situational awareness: if latency compensation (predictive rendering, extrapolation) is imperfect, operator may make unsafe decisions; human factors testing required.
evidence_needed: Detailed timing analysis and latency budget allocation for network, vehicle, and operator console components.
Trade study on network technology (5G, satellite, terrestrial) with latency, cost, availability, and operational constraints.
Vehicle autonomy capability assessment: sensor suite, compute platform, autonomous control algorithms.
SafetyAlert protocol specification with latency overhead analysis and guaranteed delivery guarantees.
Operator training and worksite safety plan updates to accommodate autonomous mode transitions.
Instrumented teleoperation testing to validate p95 ≤ 150 ms latency under representative network conditions.
Human factors study on operator situational awareness and decision-making under latency compensation.
